title: Tephrosia littoralis
intro: Tephrosia littoralis, also known as Cape bee plant or Kaffir-bean, is a flowering plant species native to South Africa, belonging to the Fabaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, and its extracts have been found to exhibit various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help in treating conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Tephrosia littoralis include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into traditional remedies such as tonics and salves.
taxonomy: Tephrosia littoralis is a species of flowering plant in the pea family (Fabaceae). It belongs to the genus Tephrosia, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as horsefly pea or horsefly vine. The species is further classified under the subgenus Tephrosia, which is one of the 27 subgenera of the genus. The plant is native to various regions including Australia and Southeast Asia, and is often found in coastal areas. It is a climber or shrub, typically growing up to 3 meters in height.
uses: Tephrosia littoralis is known to have several medicinal uses, primarily in traditional medicine. Its roots and leaves are used to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and respiratory problems. The plant is also used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and dermatitis, due to its anti-inflammatory properties. In addition, Tephrosia littoralis is believed to have antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, which can help protect against infections and diseases. More research is needed to fully understand the potential health benefits of this plant.

side_effects: Tephrosia littoralis, a plant native to South Africa, is known to be toxic and has been reported to cause side effects in some individuals. The plant's toxicity is largely due to its high concentration of pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which can be harmful if ingested. Prolonged exposure to these alkaloids can lead to liver damage and other health complications. There is limited information available on the specific side effects of Tephrosia littoralis, but it is generally recommended to avoid handling or consuming the plant.
